title: Methods and properties that don't access instance data should be static
description: Methods and properties that don’t access instance data should be marked as static for the following reasons: Clarity and Intent: Marking a method/property as static makes it clear that the method does not depend on instance data and can be called without creating an instance of the class. This improves the readability of the code by clearly conveying the member’s intended use. Performance: Instance methods/properties in C# require an instance of the class to be called.
recommendation: Refactor to eliminate the flagged pattern.

logic.reasoning: This rule identifies methods and properties that don't access instance data and could be made static for better performance and clarity. The pattern matches non-static, non-virtual methods that don't use 'this.', instance fields, or base class members. It excludes polymorphic methods, interface implementations, event handlers, and methods that access instance data. The detection focuses on utility methods that perform computations only on parameters.

should_trigger: public int Add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
public string FormatCurrency(decimal amount) { return amount.ToString("C"); }
should_not_trigger: public static int Add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
public virtual string Process(string input) { return input.ToUpper(); }
public override string Process(string input) { return input.ToLower(); }
public double AddToLastResult(double value) { lastResult += value; return lastResult; }
public void OnClick(object sender, EventArgs e) { Console.WriteLine("Clicked"); }
